lesson.menu_desc: Planning your goals & travels with future tenses -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 3 -- A Fresh Chapter Ahead
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 3 -- A Fresh Chapter Ahead

reading.rtitle: The Bucket List Reinvented: Why Smart Travelers Plan Backwards
paras: For years, the bucket list was a long, hopeful jumble of dreams -- see the Northern Lights, learn to surf, walk a famous trail. By this time next decade, travel coaches argue, most people will have abandoned that messy approach for something far more deliberate. The new idea is simple but powerful: instead of listing everything you might do, you set your sights on a single, attainable goal and you map out the route backwards from it.

The logic is compelling. A traveler who decides, today, that by next spring they will have walked a long-overdue pilgrimage route has already turned a vague aspiration into a plan. From that fixed point, every smaller decision -- the savings, the training, the time off -- falls into place. This time next year, the coaches say, you will not be dreaming about the trip. You will be living it.

There is a deeper reward, too. People who plan this way report that they broaden their horizons not by doing more, but by doing it more fully. They seize the moment instead of endlessly postponing it. A fresh start, it turns out, rarely begins with a long list. It begins with one clear destination on the horizon -- and the courage to walk toward it.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel column, Journeys Quarterly

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about planning your travels?
choices: The best travelers keep their bucket lists as long and ambitious as possible.
Choosing one clear, attainable goal and planning backwards from it is more powerful than a long wish list.
Careful planning takes the joy out of travel, so it is better not to plan at all.

items: The writer thinks the old, messy bucket list is the best approach.
f
The writer says most people will have abandoned it for something more deliberate.
The new method is to set your sights on a single, attainable goal.
t
The text says exactly this.
Planning backwards helps the smaller decisions fall into place.
t
From the fixed goal, savings, training and time off fall into place.
The writer believes a fresh start usually begins with a very long list.
f
It rarely begins with a long list -- it begins with one clear destination.
The writer suggests choosing one clear goal instead of a long list. Which single travel or life goal would you set your sights on first?
By this time next year, what do you hope you will have done that you keep postponing?
The article talks about broadening your horizons by doing things more fully, not by doing more. What does that mean to you?
What is one long-overdue plan you would love to map out and finally seize?

followup.text: Follow-up: The writer chose one clear goal and planned backwards from it. Tell your teacher one goal you would set your sights on first -- and what you will have done by this time next year to reach it.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each plan using the future tense in brackets
items: At 9 a.m. tomorrow, I am in the middle of my trip.
will be
I plan to finish the course before December.
will have
Next summer, we are travelling around Italy.
will be
By next year, I reach my savings goal.
will have
This time next month, she is walking the Camino.
will be

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: At 9 a.m. tomorrow, I will be travelling.
I will have finished the course before December.
Next summer, we will be travelling around Italy.
By next year, I will have reached my savings goal.
This time next month, she will be walking the Camino.
answerkey.note: Form check: future continuous = will be + -ing (in progress at a future time); future perfect = will have + past participle (finished before a future time).

analyse.title: How the writer makes the future feel real
items: Find a sentence that uses the future perfect (will have...) to make a future result feel certain.
The writer says 'you will not be dreaming about the trip -- you will be living it'. Why is that contrast so persuasive?
In your own words, what is the single most important idea the writer wants you to take away?

extra_audio.text: First, retirement opens a long-overdue fresh start. Then she writes the dreams that are top of her bucket list. Next, she maps out an attainable plan and sets her sights on Japan. By the time she boards, she will have finished her English course. This time next year, she will be travelling and broadening her horizons.

listenings.text: So here is how I am thinking about the year ahead. This time next year, I will not be sitting at home wondering what to do. I will be travelling. I have set my sights on one clear goal: by next spring, I will have finally walked a long-overdue trail I have dreamed about for years. Everything else falls into place around that. I will be saving a little each month, I will be training on the weekends, and I will be planning the route step by step. It is not a vague aspiration anymore. It is a plan with a date. And honestly, I cannot wait to seize the moment and make the most of this fresh chapter.

People ask me what I will be doing in five years, and I always smile, because I genuinely do not know all the details. But I do know the direction. By then, I will have broadened my horizons in ways I cannot fully picture yet. I will be exploring places that are still uncharted for me, and I will have ticked off at least three goals I keep postponing. The trick, I have learned, is to map out the big destinations and stay flexible about everything else. A fresh start does not need a perfect plan. It just needs one clear goal on the horizon and the courage to begin.
